lotl.label: What's Going On With the Leafs
lotl.body: The Maple Leafs' season ended not with a bang but a whimper—a seven-game losing streak that sealed their fate and sent them packing with a 32-36-14 record, eight points back in the Atlantic Division and sitting 15th in the playoff picture. Toronto's collapse down the stretch left them 35 games behind the division leader, a chasm that speaks to deeper structural problems than any single trade deadline move could fix. The front office now faces the hard reckoning that comes with missing the postseason: coaching decisions loom, free agency looms larger, and the draft lottery becomes the season's only remaining lifeline. This team finished exactly where its inconsistency demanded—out of contention, out of time, and out of excuses. The real work begins now in the offseason, where management must decide whether to retool around the core or tear it down and start fresh. For a franchise that's made the playoffs 18 straight years before this, the silence of April is deafening.

the_latest.headline: Why The NHL's 'Ball-By-Ball' Draft Lottery Will Be A Must-Watch For Maple Leafs Fans
NHLPA poll: Sidney Crosby most likely to become head coach
the_latest.dek: Sidney Crosby is seen by his peers as the NHL player most likely to become a head coach, according to the National Hockey League Players' Association's annual player poll released Friday.

the_latest.dek: The Avalanche and Lightning are tied for the longest active consecutive playoff streaks in the NHL. See which other teams make the list.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Jays
lotl.body: The Blue Jays' early-season dreams are curdling into a familiar sour mash — another road trip, another gut punch in the desert. Jeff Hoffman's grand slam implosion to Corbin Carroll in the eighth handed Arizona a 6-2 win Friday night, capping a 12-hit Blue Jays tease that stranded eight and scored just two. That's four straight losses now, from Milwaukee's late daggers to this Diamondbacks double dip, dropping Toronto to 7-13 — dead last in the AL East, 5 games back at playoff seed 13. The bats are flickering but fizzling; the bullpen's a dumpster fire. No fresh injury buzz cuts through the haze, but this skid screams urgency for a lineup that's punching above its weight in chances, not runs. John Schneider's crew can't afford more late-inning heartbreaks if they're clawing back into contention. Today's rubber match at 3:10 PM ET sends Kevin Gausman to the hill — desperate for that series split, or the hole digs deeper.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Commanders
lotl.body: The Washington Commanders stand at a crossroads—a 5-12 season, buried third in the NFC East, left them nine games behind the division leader and staring down one of the most consequential drafts in franchise history. With the No. 7 overall pick and potential trade scenarios swirling around them, the front office is tasked with fortifying a roster that hemorrhaged talent in free agency. Deebo Samuel, Austin Ekeler, Von Miller, and Zach Ertz are gone—the cupboard stripped bare. Jayden Daniels needs weapons, and the mock drafts circulating this week suggest the Commanders are eyeing elite playmakers on both sides of the ball. One scenario has them trading down and acquiring an additional first-rounder, pivoting toward a receiver like the Biletnikoff Award winner while addressing a secondary that's been a chronic liability. Jermod McCoy, a shutdown corner still recovering from a torn ACL, represents the kind of upside pick that could define this offseason. The draft convenes next week, and every decision made in the war room will either accelerate the rebuild or extend the suffering. The Commanders have no room for missteps.
